skill.name: google-agents-cli-eval
skill.description: Guides users through evaluating Google ADK agents with agents-cli. It covers dataset design, metric selection, trace grading, failure analysis, user simulation, and prompt optimization.
skill.summary: Evaluate Google ADK agents with agents-cli datasets, metrics, grading, and failure analysis.
skill.author: google

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command execution, weak crypto, environment, and network patterns. Review found the command and weak-crypto alerts are mainly Markdown command examples, schema examples, and inline identifiers, not executable skill code. The confirmed risk is medium because the skill instructs agents to run external CLI workflows, call managed Google evaluation services, and use local custom metric code that can execute with CLI privileges.

medium_findings.title: Local Custom Metric Code Executes With CLI Privileges
medium_findings.description: The skill documents CodeExecutionMetric custom_function blocks and explicitly notes that local execution runs with the CLI process privileges. This is legitimate evaluation functionality, but users must only run trusted metric code because it can access local files, credentials, and network resources available to the CLI.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly describes local custom metric execution and states it uses CLI privileges. The risk depends on user-supplied metric code, so it is a strong warning rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Managed Evaluation Workflows May Send Data To Google Services
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends dataset synthesis, server-side grading, and managed eval submission workflows. These are intended product features, but evaluation datasets and traces can contain sensitive prompts, tool outputs, or agent behavior, so users need data governance before using cloud-side commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files clearly describe server-side scenario generation and managed eval submission. This is expected network behavior, but sensitive trace data could be transmitted if users run those workflows.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Alerts Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The numerous Ruby or shell backtick findings correspond to inline Markdown code, fenced command examples, and agents-cli usage instructions. No executable script, hook, or bundled program in the reviewed files runs those commands automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are documentation text and fenced examples. The skill consists of Markdown reference material, so these static matches are false positives for automatic command execution.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Example Uses A Mock API Key Pattern
low_findings.description: The environment access finding points to sample Python documentation that reads EXTERNAL_API_KEY and returns a mock response when the key is missing or set to a dummy value. I did not find evidence that the skill exfiltrates or stores secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code is a documented mock-mode example and contains no outbound request in the shown block. It still teaches credential handling, so it remains a minor review note.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings are public documentation links to SDK source and Google or uv documentation. They are references for users, not hidden network endpoints or exfiltration destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible Markdown links to expected documentation. No code path automatically calls these URLs.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local CodeExecutionMetric custom_function
dangerous_patterns.description: Custom metric functions can execute Python locally through the evaluation CLI. This is powerful but should be limited to trusted repositories and reviewed metric code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly documented and security-relevant. It is not malicious by itself because the skill warns that local execution uses CLI privileges.

dangerous_patterns.title: External agents-cli Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run agents-cli eval commands that read datasets, write traces and results, and may contact Google services depending on the subcommand.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are clear and numerous. They are intended behavior for this skill, so the concern is operational risk rather than malicious code execution.

content.user_title: Evaluate ADK Agents with Google Agents CLI
content.value_statement: Agent evaluations are hard to structure, grade, and improve consistently. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical guidance for agents-cli eval workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains the Google Agents CLI quality flywheel for preparing data, generating traces, grading, analyzing, and optimizing agents.
Shows how to run agents-cli eval generate, grade, compare, analyze, dataset synthesize, submit, and results commands.
Maps common evaluation failures to practical fixes for prompts, tools, orchestration, grounding, and safety.
Documents dataset formats for single-turn, multi-turn, multi-agent, tool-call, and multimodal evaluation cases.
Guides metric selection across task success, trajectory quality, tool use, hallucination, safety, and custom judge metrics.
Explains limitations of built-in tools, Google Search grounding, user simulation, and multimodal grading.
limitations: Requires agents-cli and a compatible Google ADK project to run commands.
Some workflows call managed Google evaluation services and may need GCP project configuration.
Local custom metric functions run with the CLI process privileges and must be trusted.
Command flags and managed service defaults can change, so users should check command help for current options.

use_cases.title: Build Regression Evals
use_cases.description: Create evaluation datasets and run trace grading before changing prompts, tools, or orchestration logic.

use_cases.title: Diagnose Agent Failures
use_cases.description: Interpret metric failures, inspect traces, compare runs, and identify targeted fixes for failing cases.

use_cases.title: Scale Scenario Testing
use_cases.description: Use synthesized user scenarios and managed grading workflows for broader coverage across agent behaviors.

prompt_templates.title: Run My First Eval
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a scaffolded ADK project
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate my ADK agent with agents-cli. Start with the scaffolded dataset, run the basic eval loop, and explain the results.

prompt_templates.title: Choose Metrics
prompt_templates.scenario: Selecting metrics for a specific agent goal
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose evaluation metrics for my ADK agent. The agent goal is [describe goal], and I need to catch [failure types].

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Failures
prompt_templates.scenario: Debugging poor scores after grading traces
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these agents-cli eval results and trace summaries. Identify likely root causes and propose the next fixes to test.

prompt_templates.title: Design Advanced Coverage
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced multi-turn or multimodal evaluation planning
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a complete evaluation plan for my multi-turn ADK agent, including datasets, metrics, custom judges, and compare criteria.

output_examples.input: My agent fails tool_use_quality after adding a search tool.
output: Explains whether the tool is model-internal or function-based.
Recommends metrics that can evaluate visible tool calls correctly.
Suggests trace inspection steps and prompt or tool-description fixes.

output_examples.input: I need an eval dataset for a booking assistant.
output: Describes the right single-turn or multi-turn dataset shape.
Lists required fields and common schema mistakes to avoid.
Suggests metrics for task success, tool use, hallucination, and safety.

output_examples.input: My generated image eval is not checking the image content.
output: Explains why built-in text metrics miss media parts.
Recommends a vision-capable custom judge metric.
Notes when to use file references instead of embedded media.

best_practices: Start with a few focused cases, then expand coverage after the agent passes them reliably.
Compare every new result against a baseline to confirm improvements and detect regressions.
Use trusted code only for local custom metrics because they execute with local CLI privileges.
anti_patterns: Do not lower thresholds just to make failing evaluations pass.
Do not delete flaky cases before investigating non-determinism or weak instructions.
Do not use fixed trajectory matching for model-internal tools that do not appear in traces.

faq.question: What does this skill help evaluate?
faq.answer: It helps evaluate Google ADK agents using agents-cli datasets, traces, built-in metrics, and custom metrics.

faq.question: Does it run evaluations by itself?
faq.answer: It provides guidance and command patterns. The user still needs agents-cli and a suitable ADK project.

faq.question: Can it evaluate multi-turn agents?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains multi-turn datasets, user simulation, trajectory metrics, and task success grading.

faq.question: Can it evaluate multimodal inputs?
faq.answer: Yes, but true multimodal grading usually needs a custom LLM judge with a vision-capable model.

faq.question: Are custom metrics safe?
faq.answer: LLM judge metrics are prompt based. Local code metrics should only use trusted code because they run with CLI privileges.

faq.question: Which tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report marks the skill as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
